author: Eusebius of Caesarea
note: The Papias tradition on the origin of Mark's Gospel. This is a chain of testimony: Eusebius (c. 325) quotes Papias of Hierapolis (writing c. 95–110), who in turn reports what 'the presbyter' (traditionally John the Elder) said. It is the earliest external witness that Mark wrote from Peter's preaching — apologetically load-bearing for the Gospel-as-eyewitness-derived case. Store as historical testimony, not as proof; note that 'not in order' is Papias's own qualification and that scholars debate whether 'the presbyter' is John the apostle or a distinct John the Elder. Seed entry — verify verbatim vs source_url in the citations pass before publishing.
text: This also the presbyter said: Mark, having become the interpreter of Peter, wrote down accurately, though not in order, whatsoever he remembered of the things said or done by Christ. For he neither heard the Lord nor followed him, but afterward, as I said, he followed Peter, who adapted his teaching to the needs of his hearers, but with no intention of giving a connected account of the Lord's discourses, so that Mark committed no error while he thus wrote some things as he remembered them. For he was careful of one thing, not to omit any of the things which he had heard, and not to state any of them falsely.

author: Eusebius of Caesarea
note: The Papias tradition on Matthew — the earliest external statement that Matthew composed the 'oracles' (logia) in the Hebrew (i.e. Hebrew/Aramaic) tongue. Store as historical testimony via Eusebius (c. 325) quoting Papias (c. 95–110). Handle with care in essays: 'the oracles' (ta logia) and 'in the Hebrew language' are scholarly cruxes — it is debated whether Papias means our Greek Matthew, an Aramaic sayings-source, or a Hebrew style of composition. Do not overstate it as a simple proof of Matthean authorship of canonical Greek Matthew. Seed entry — verify verbatim vs source_url before publishing.
text: So then Matthew wrote the oracles in the Hebrew language, and every one interpreted them as he was able.

author: Eusebius of Caesarea
note: Eusebius's summary of the 'acknowledged' (homologoumena) New Testament writings, c. 325 — the four Gospels, Acts, Paul's epistles, 1 John, 1 Peter, and (with a noted reservation) Revelation. Apologetically useful for showing a settled core canon early in the fourth century. Store with the accurate qualifier: Eusebius classes these as the recognized books, but immediately proceeds to list 'disputed' (antilegomena) and 'rejected' books — so quote this as evidence for the stable CORE, not as a claim that the full 27-book canon was undebated. His own hesitation over the Apocalypse ('if it really seem proper') should be represented, not smoothed over. Seed entry — verify verbatim vs source_url before publishing.
text: Since we are dealing with this subject it is proper to sum up the writings of the New Testament which have been already mentioned. First then must be put the holy quaternion of the Gospels; following them the Acts of the Apostles. After this must be reckoned the epistles of Paul; next in order the extant former epistle of John, and likewise the epistle of Peter, must be maintained. After them is to be placed, if it really seem proper, the Apocalypse of John, concerning which we shall give the different opinions at the proper time. These then belong among the accepted writings.

author: Eusebius of Caesarea
note: IMPORTANT / handle with care. Eusebius here attributes to Josephus a saying that the fall of Jerusalem avenged the murder of James the Just, 'a brother of Jesus, that is called the Christ.' This exact sentence is NOT found in the surviving manuscripts of Josephus; both Eusebius and (earlier) Origen cite it, but scholars regard it as a non-genuine addition — it is not the authentic James reference. The genuine, widely-accepted Josephus notice of James is Antiquities 20.9.1 (20.200): 'the brother of Jesus who was called Christ, whose name was James.' A future essay should cite the authentic Antiquities 20.200 passage for the historical James, and use THIS Eusebian quotation only as evidence of early Christian tradition ABOUT Josephus — never present it as Josephus's own verified words. Related caution: Eusebius's fuller Josephus 'Testimonium Flavianum' (HE 1.11 / Antiquities 18.3.3) is widely regarded as containing later Christian interpolation and must be quoted only in a carefully qualified form. Seed entry — verify verbatim vs source_url before publishing.
text: Josephus, at least, has not hesitated to testify this in his writings, where he says, 'These things happened to the Jews to avenge James the Just, who was a brother of Jesus, that is called the Christ. For the Jews slew him, although he was a most just man.'

author: Eusebius of Caesarea
note: Polycarp of Smyrna's reply to the proconsul, quoted by Eusebius (c. 325) from the letter of the church of Smyrna (the Martyrdom of Polycarp, c. 155–160). A short, vivid witness to the steadfastness of the martyrs — apologetically useful as evidence of sincere conviction unto death, not as independent proof of any doctrinal claim. Store as historical testimony; note that Eusebius is quoting an earlier document, and that 'eighty-six years' likely dates Polycarp's Christian life, not simply his age. Seed entry — verify verbatim vs source_url before publishing.
text: But when the magistrate pressed him, and said, 'Swear, and I will release thee; revile Christ,' Polycarp said, 'Fourscore and six years have I been serving him, and he hath done me no wrong; how then can I blaspheme my king who saved me?'
